title: SODAR (Sonic Detection and Ranging)
focus: Function and applications of SODAR in atmospheric monitoring.
rationale: Small standalone topic on atmospheric monitoring technology.
sectionExcerpt: SODAR works by sending sound pulses and subsequently monitoring the intensity and frequency of the returned signal. Specifically, it determines the lower-atmosphere wind profile and temperature structure. Provides information on height of inversion, height of fog layer, strength of turbulence etc.
evidenceBullets: Developed by CSIR-AMPRI, Bhopal.
Sends sound pulses to monitor intensity and frequency of returned signals.
Determines lower-atmosphere wind profile and temperature structure.
Provides data on inversion height, fog layer height, and turbulence strength.

title: HANSA-3 (NG) Trainer Aircraft
focus: Features and significance of the HANSA-3 (NG) trainer plane.
rationale: Specific aviation technology transfer and development by CSIR-NAL.
sectionExcerpt: CSIR-National Aerospace Laboratories (NAL) signs technology transfer deal with a private firm to manufacture HANSA trainer-planes. Powered by an advanced fuel-efficient Rotax 912 iSc3 Sports engine.
evidenceBullets: Technology transfer deal signed by CSIR-NAL to manufacture HANSA trainer-planes.
Powered by a fuel-efficient Rotax 912 iSc3 Sports engine.
Aims to strengthen India's aviation industry and flying training ecosystem.

title: KAVACH Automatic Train Protection System
focus: Mechanism and features of the indigenous Kavach train safety system.
rationale: Highly important indigenous safety technology for Indian Railways, frequently tested in UPSC.
sectionExcerpt: Indigenously Automatic Train Protection system designed to improve train safety and efficiency. The Kavach assists loco pilots by displaying signals in the train and automatically applying the brakes if the pilot fails to do so. Radio Frequency Identification (RFID) tags are placed throughout the entire track length to enable the Kavach to track train positions and detect any potential safety issues along the route.
evidenceBullets: Indigenous Automatic Train Protection system.
Automatically applies brakes if the loco pilot fails to do so.
Operates smoothly in extreme weather conditions like fog.
Uses Radio Frequency Identification (RFID) tags along the track to track train positions.

title: Shinkansen Technology
focus: Features of Japan's high-speed bullet train technology being introduced in India.
rationale: Specific high-speed rail technology relevant to the Mumbai-Ahmedabad Bullet train project.
sectionExcerpt: Next generation E10 Shinkansen trains are to be introduced simultaneously in India (Mumbai-Ahmedabad Bullet train project) and Japan. It is Japan's High-speed bullet train. Key Features: Dedicated track for high-speed rail that is free of level crossings and ATC system (Automatic Train Control System).
evidenceBullets: Japan's high-speed bullet train technology.
Next-generation E10 trains to be used in the Mumbai-Ahmedabad Bullet train project.
Features dedicated tracks free of level crossings and an Automatic Train Control (ATC) system.
Includes aerodynamic optimization for vehicle noise reduction.

title: Black Box (Flight Recorders)
focus: Purpose, components, and physical characteristics of aircraft Black Boxes.
rationale: Crucial aviation safety device with specific factual details (color, components, materials) suitable for MCQs.
sectionExcerpt: Records data during flights and crucial information on plane crashes. Comprise two Critical Flight Recording Devices: Flight Data Recorder and Cockpit Voice Recorder. Colour: Bright orange to ensure high visibility. Material: Made of strong substances (steel or titanium) and are insulated from extreme heat or cold.
evidenceBullets: Records flight data and crucial crash information.
Comprises a Flight Data Recorder and a Cockpit Voice Recorder.
Painted bright orange for high visibility, not black.
Made of strong materials like steel or titanium and insulated against extreme temperatures.

title: Altermagnets
focus: Definition, properties, and duality of altermagnets compared to ferro and antiferromagnets.
rationale: Novel scientific discovery in materials science with clear comparative properties ideal for conceptual questions.
sectionExcerpt: Altermagnets are a new type of magnetic material that combine the best features of ferromagnets and antiferromagnets. Like antiferromagnets, they exhibit alternating spins and therefore have no net magnetism. But due to their unique crystal symmetry, they show intense internal spin polarisation, similar to ferromagnets.
evidenceBullets: Discovered by scientists from S.N. Bose National Centre for Basic Sciences using chromium antimonide (CrSb).
Combine features of ferromagnets (intense internal spin polarization) and antiferromagnets (alternating spins, no net magnetism).
CrSb exhibits the strongest spin-splitting among known altermagnets.

title: Accelerometer
focus: Principle of functioning and applications of accelerometers.
rationale: Common sensor technology with diverse applications (earthquakes, smartphones, airbags) that UPSC often tests in everyday science.
sectionExcerpt: A sensing device that measures acceleration and detects the frequency and intensity of human movement. Accelerometers work according to Newton's law of motion, the force on an object is equal to its mass times its acceleration. Can measure both: Static acceleration (due to gravity) & dynamic acceleration (car crash). Key Applications: smartphones, Laptops, digital cameras... Vehicles: Used to trigger airbags. Drones: to stabilize their orientation mid-flight.
evidenceBullets: Measures both static acceleration (gravity) and dynamic acceleration (movement/crashes).
Works on Newton's law of motion (force equals mass times acceleration).
Used in Google's Android Earthquake Alert system to detect earthquakes.
Applications include rotating smartphone displays, triggering vehicle airbags, and stabilizing drones.

title: Desalination Technologies
focus: Comparison between Thermal and Membrane desalination technologies and their applications.
rationale: Environmental and water technology topic with clear distinctions between thermal (LTTD) and membrane (RO) methods.
sectionExcerpt: Thermal Technology: Heating of saline water and collecting the condensed vapor (distillate) to produce pure water. Usage: Mainly for seawater desalination E.g., Low Temperature Thermal Desalination (LTTD) plants, Lakshadweep. Membrane Technology: Feedwater is pumped through semi-permeable membranes to filter out the dissolved solids. Usage: Mainly for brackish water desalination E.g., Nemmeli Seawater Desalination Plant, Tamil Nadu, on Reverse Osmosis.
evidenceBullets: Thermal technology heats saline water and collects condensed vapor, mainly used for seawater (e.g., LTTD in Lakshadweep).
Membrane technology pumps feedwater through semi-permeable membranes to filter dissolved solids, mainly for brackish water.
Nemmeli Seawater Desalination Plant in Tamil Nadu uses Reverse Osmosis and is the largest in South Asia.
IIT Bombay developed hydrophobic Graphene-based lotus leaf-like solar evaporators for desalination.

title: Advanced Driver Assistance System (ADAS)
focus: Mandate and application of ADAS in commercial vehicles.
rationale: Brief mention of a policy/technology mandate for vehicle safety.
sectionExcerpt: Union Government is considering mandating Advanced Driver Assistance System (ADAS) in all new models of trucks and buses, including school buses.
evidenceBullets: The Union Government is considering mandating ADAS in all new models of trucks and buses, including school buses.
